IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles an important problem—disambiguating causal lexical markers in context—and demonstrates empirical gains over the prior SOTA (Hidey & McKeown 2016) on the AltLex corpus, so its results are useful to practitioners working on discourse/causality. However, impact is moderated by methodological gaps emphasized from my perspective: only two baselines (most-freq and the SVM from Hidey & McKeown) are compared rather than the three diverse baselines I expect (including a strong linguistically-motivated non-neural baseline), and there is no probing or ablation to isolate the contribution of pre-trained embeddings. Given dataset limitations noted by the authors and the lack of a formal linguistic/causal model explaining why an LSTM-based encoder succeeds, the work is a solid, buildable contribution but not likely to fundamentally change how researchers think about causality detection. | B(3): This paper tackles a useful applied problem—disambiguating causal lexical markers in context—which matters for discourse and information extraction and is evaluated on the established AltLex corpus. It shows practical empirical gains by applying LSTM encoders with GloVe embeddings to outperform the prior SVM-based state-of-the-art (Hidey & McKeown 2016), which is valuable for practitioners. However, the contribution is largely an application of standard sequence models rather than a conceptual shift, so it is unlikely to change how the field thinks about causality broadly; it is a moderate-impact, reproducible advance likely to be built upon in related NLP tasks.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—disambiguating causal lexical markers in context—and demonstrates empirical gains over the prior SOTA (Hidey & McKeown 2016) on the AltLex corpus, so its results are useful to practitioners working on discourse/causality. However, impact is moderated by methodological gaps emphasized from my perspective: only two baselines (most-freq and the SVM from Hidey & McKeown) are compared rather than the three diverse baselines I expect (including a strong linguistically-motivated non-neural baseline), and there is no probing or ablation to isolate the contribution of pre-trained embeddings. Given dataset limitations noted by the authors and the lack of a formal linguistic/causal model explaining why an LSTM-based encoder succeeds, the work is a solid, buildable contribution but not likely to fundamentally change how researchers think about causality detection.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles a useful applied problem—disambiguating causal lexical markers in context—which matters for discourse and information extraction and is evaluated on the established AltLex corpus. It shows practical empirical gains by applying LSTM encoders with GloVe embeddings to outperform the prior SVM-based state-of-the-art (Hidey & McKeown 2016), which is valuable for practitioners. However, the contribution is largely an application of standard sequence models rather than a conceptual shift, so it is unlikely to change how the field thinks about causality broadly; it is a moderate-impact, reproducible advance likely to be built upon in related NLP tasks.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is overall well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (neural architecture for disambiguating causal markers and the need for contextual encoding). Notation and experimental setup (datasets, baselines, metrics, hyperparameters) are reported consistently, and the writing quality is described as good with adequate related work coverage. Minor presentation issues remain — for example, code availability is not mentioned and the summary does not confirm whether figures/tables provide deep illustrative error analyses — preventing an exceptional rating. | B(4): The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early—demonstrating an LSTM + GloVe approach outperforming Hidey and McKeown (2016) on the AltLex corpus. Notation and experimental details (including hyperparameters) are reported, and related work coverage is adequate, which supports reproducibility. Figures/tables and examples (e.g., datasets, baselines, precision/recall results) appear informative, and overall writing quality is good, with only minor presentation issues that prevent it from being exceptional.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is overall well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (neural architecture for disambiguating causal markers and the need for contextual encoding). Notation and experimental setup (datasets, baselines, metrics, hyperparameters) are reported consistently, and the writing quality is described as good with adequate related work coverage. Minor presentation issues remain — for example, code availability is not mentioned and the summary does not confirm whether figures/tables provide deep illustrative error analyses — preventing an exceptional rating.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early—demonstrating an LSTM + GloVe approach outperforming Hidey and McKeown (2016) on the AltLex corpus. Notation and experimental details (including hyperparameters) are reported, and related work coverage is adequate, which supports reproducibility. Figures/tables and examples (e.g., datasets, baselines, precision/recall results) appear informative, and overall writing quality is good, with only minor presentation issues that prevent it from being exceptional.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is on-topic for a top NLP venue and uses appropriate datasets and evaluation; they also both note the contribution is primarily empirical and somewhat incremental. I give a 4 because the empirical gains and relevance to discourse/causality make it a solid fit, while acknowledging the paper would be stronger with deeper linguistic analysis, additional non-neural baselines/ablations, and clearer reproducibility (e.g., code release).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an NLP problem (disambiguating causal lexical markers) squarely within top-tier conference scope and uses standard neural methods and corpora (AltLex), so the audience and evaluation metrics are appropriate. However, from the summary it lacks the kind of formal/linguistic analysis I expect for this topic (no explicit causal/semantic model) and compares to only two baselines (most-frequent and one prior SVM), missing a strong non-neural or linguistically-motivated baseline and ablations that probe the role of pre-trained embeddings. Given these omissions the work is borderline: relevant to the venue but would need stronger theoretical positioning and more diverse baselines/analysis to be a clear fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is a good fit for a top NLP/ML conference: it tackles an applied NLP problem (causal lexical marker disambiguation) using standard, well-understood neural components (LSTMs + GloVe) and reports empirical gains over a prior SOTA on the AltLex corpus. The methodology and evaluation (precision/recall, comparison to a strong SVM baseline using lexical resources) match community expectations, and the work will interest researchers working on discourse/causality and sequence models. I dock one point because the contribution is largely empirical and incremental rather than introducing new modeling paradigms, and code release is not mentioned which would strengthen reproducibility.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper applies standard neural components (LSTMs + GloVe) to an existing causality-disambiguation task and represents an incremental systems improvement rather than a fundamentally new method or task. Given the clear empirical gains over the prior SVM baseline but lack of novel modelling or theoretical contribution, a middle score reflects solid empirical work with limited originality.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies standard neural ingredients (LSTM sequence encoders + GloVe embeddings) to an existing causality-disambiguation task (AltLex) and reports improvements over the Hidey & McKeown (2016) SVM baseline; this is an application of well-known architectures rather than a genuinely new method. The problem formulation (contextual disambiguation of causal markers) is a recognised, well-studied instance of discourse/semantic classification rather than a novel task definition, and the paper does not introduce a formal or linguistic model that would constitute a new perspective. From the standpoint of a theoretical linguist, the contribution reads as an incremental systems advance—useful empirically but unlikely to surprise or substantially inform experts about new mechanisms for causality interpretation.

reviewer_b.justification: This work applies established sequence modelling (LSTM) and pre-trained GloVe embeddings to the task of disambiguating causal lexical markers, outperforming the prior SVM-based system on the AltLex corpus. The problem itself (causal vs non-causal marker classification) is well-studied, and the model is a straightforward neuralization of prior feature-based approaches rather than a fundamentally new method. The paper clearly differentiates from Hidey and McKeown (2016) and demonstrates empirical gains, so it represents a solid incremental advance rather than a highly novel idea.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper shows solid empirical gains over prior SOTA and reports key evaluation metrics and hyperparameters, but is weakened by missing ablation/sensitivity analyses and lack of released code or deeper qualitative/probing analyses. Given the clear empirical contribution but limited depth in analysis and reproducibility resources, a middle score reflects meaningful progress that nonetheless needs further experiments and transparency to be fully convincing.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments demonstrate improved performance over the prior SVM system and a majority baseline, but they lack the depth I expect: no ablation studies isolating the contribution of LSTM context encoding versus embeddings are reported, and there are only two baselines (no strong linguistically-motivated, non-neural baseline or additional diverse comparisons). Related work coverage is said to be adequate and some limitations are acknowledged, but there is no evidence of thorough qualitative error analysis or probing of pre-trained embeddings’ role; reproducibility is partially supported (hyperparameters reported) but code is not mentioned. From my perspective these omissions leave important causal and linguistic questions unexamined, so the evaluation is shallow.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents solid empirical evidence that an LSTM+GloVe model outperforms the prior SOTA (Hidey & McKeown 2016) on both AltLex corpus variants, and reports hyperparameters and two evaluation metrics (precision/recall), which supports the main claims. It discusses relevant limitations (corpus size, lexical-resource coverage, embedding biases) and situates itself against a reasonable baseline (most-frequent) and a strong prior SVM system. Missing, however, are explicit ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., impact of embedding choice, LSTM vs simpler encoders) and no code release is mentioned, which would aid reproducibility and deeper inspection. Overall the experimental work is strong and practical, but would benefit from those additional analyses and resources to be fully comprehensive.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the experiments support the main claim and that hyperparameters and evaluation on two splits are reported, but both note missing statistical significance testing and limited reproducibility (no public code). Reviewer A additionally highlights the lack of stronger baselines and ablation studies, while Reviewer B finds the methodology otherwise reasonable. Given the supported empirical results but important methodological omissions, a moderate score of 3 reflects sound but incomplete validation.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claim—that an LSTM+GloVe model can disambiguate causal lexical markers and outperforms Hidey & McKeown (2016) on the AltLex corpus—is supported by experiments, hyperparameters, and evaluation on both non-bootstrapped and bootstrapped splits. However, from a technical-reviewer perspective important methodological gaps remain: the paper compares only to a most-frequent baseline and a single SVM system (no linguistically-motivated or non-neural strong baselines), does not report any ablations isolating the contribution of pre-trained embeddings, and lacks mention of statistical significance testing. Given these omissions and the absence of a formal/causal model or in-depth qualitative error analysis that I require, the work is promising but incomplete for a higher technical score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments showing the LSTM+GloVe model outperforms the Hidey and McKeown (2016) SVM baseline and a most-frequent baseline on both AltLex versions, with hyperparameters reported. Experimental methodology is mostly sound—appropriate baselines and sensible metrics (precision/recall) are used—but I see no mention of statistical significance testing or public code release, which weakens reproducibility. There are no obvious mathematical errors (no heavy theory), and stated limitations acknowledge data and resource-coverage issues, so the conclusions are reasonable though minor reproducibility concerns remain.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes fair and relevant comparisons to the prior SOTA and a majority baseline on the same AltLex corpus with standard metrics, so experimental comparisons are sound. However, they also agree the evaluation is not fully comprehensive—missing additional diverse baselines, recent neural comparisons, explicit ablations/probes, and more detailed breakdowns—so while the comparison is strong it falls short of being exhaustive, meriting a 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a reasonable strong prior (Hidey & McKeown 2016 SVM) and a majority baseline, and evaluates on the same AltLex data with standard metrics — so core comparisons are present and fair. However, from the summary I see only two baselines (plus MFC); my reviewer persona expects at least three diverse baselines including stronger non-neural/linguistically-motivated methods and explicit ablations or probes isolating the role of pre-trained embeddings, which are missing. The manuscript also does not report detailed analysis showing when prior methods are competitive or an explicit breakdown of differences vs. baselines, so comparative analysis is incomplete despite adequate related-work coverage.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against sensible baselines: a most-frequent-class baseline and the prior state-of-the-art (Hidey & McKeown, 2016) SVM system with lexical-resource features, evaluated on the same AltLex corpus variants using Precision/Recall. Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics) and the authors acknowledge limitations and when resource-based methods suffer (coverage/recall), and they analyze why embedding + LSTM helps for contextual disambiguation. Minor omissions remain—no mention of more recent neural baselines or ablation studies and code release would improve reproducibility—so the experimental comparison is strong but not fully comprehensive.

main_claims: A neural network architecture can disambiguate causal lexical markers based on context.
Encoding sentence/contextual meaning is required to disambiguate causal vs non-causal uses of lexical markers.
The proposed model, using word embeddings and LSTM-based context encoding, outperforms the prior state-of-the-art (Hidey and McKeown, 2016) on the AltLex corpus.
_summary.methodology: A neural network architecture (LSTM RNNs to encode sequential/contextual information) is fed pre-trained word embedding vectors (GloVe 300d) to classify instances of lexical markers as Causal or Non-Causal.
datasets: AltLex corpus (two versions: non-bootstrapping and bootstrapping)
baselines: B2: Hidey and McKeown (2016) SVM system using features from corpus and lexical resources (WordNet, VerbNet, PropBank)
limitations: Causality classification lacks large, representative corpora covering a wide range of causal expressions.
Methods relying on external lexical resources (e.g., B2) have reduced recall limited by the coverage of those resources.
Word-embedding based methods can be biased by over-representation of individual words in one class (affecting ambiguous expressions).

reviewer_a.name: Skeptical Theorist
reviewer_a.background: Senior computational linguist with a PhD in theoretical syntax-semantics and 12+ years reviewing for ACL/EMNLP; focuses on formal models of meaning and rigorous error analysis.
traits: Penalises empirical papers that lack formal or theoretical analysis of the phenomenon (will deduct points if no explicit linguistic/causal model is provided).
Requires at least three diverse baselines (including a strong non-neural/linguistically-motivated baseline) and detailed ablation studies; deducts points if fewer are provided.
Insists on thorough qualitative error analysis and examples showing model behavior across linguistic phenomena; will lower score if analysis is superficial or absent.
Penalises reliance on pre-trained embeddings without probing their role (will deduct if no experiments isolating embedding contribution are included).
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Prioritises conceptual clarity and explanatory insight over raw empirical improvement; rewards papers that provide a formal account or deep analysis of why the model works.

reviewer_b.name: Practical ML Engineer
reviewer_b.background: Senior NLP/ML researcher in industry with extensive experience deploying sequence models; frequent reviewer for NeurIPS/ICLR and contributor to open-source toolkits.
traits: Rewards clear empirical advances on established benchmarks (gives positive weight to any statistically significant SOTA improvement).

Favors well-engineered neural solutions that use standard components (e.g., LSTMs + GloVe) when accompanied by reproducible details (public code, hyperparameters); will boost score if resources are released.
Gives credit for tackling low-resource or noisy real-world datasets and for framing useful applied tasks (rewards practical applicability).
Less concerned about formal/theoretical grounding; tolerates empirical-only papers provided experiments are solid, baselines appropriate, and results reproducible.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates primarily on empirical rigor, reproducibility, and practical impact; willing to accept incremental methodological novelty if it yields reliable improvements and resources for the community.
